segments.text: Hello and welcome to the walkthrough for the tutorial of Understanding Understanding Active Fire Detection Using Uncertainty with Bayesian Neural Networks.
This chapter is from section 6, Ecological Processes and Simulations from the Applied Artificial Intelligence in Deep Learning Learning book.
Any of the figures you are about to see in this presentation came directly from the chapter and we'll have their figure reference number indicated for you to search them for more information.
My name is Julia. I am one of the chapter authors. I'm also a forest fire modeling specialist with
the Canadian Forestry Service. My current work is around developing algorithms for
detecting and describing active fires within Canada, specifically using satellite observed
hotspots, and I also have some experience designing and training deep learning models
with a focus on the classification neural net. The objective of this chapter was to explore
application potential of deep learning in regards to active fire monitoring, specifically through
the use of satellite detected hotspots. All of the hotspot data in this tutorial came from
Beers and Moda's active fire products, which are available to download for free from NASA firms.
In the interest of computational time for this exercise, we're just going to focus on a single
day of fire activity, which will be September 23, 2003. We're also going to restrict it spatially
to Canada? By restricting our fire activity to Canada, we can use some of the available fire
perimeters, such as the National Burned Area and the Canadian National Fire Database, to assign the
classifications to the hotspots. Hotspots are classified as either positive, meaning that the
hotspot, once it has been buffered to account for satellite resolution, fell within a fire perimeter
that was recorded within one of these data sets, or it is not positive, meaning that NASA firms
is reporting a hotspot labeled as fire, but neither of these data sets support that there
was an active fire in the location at the time of detection. I would like to take this moment
to point out that although it is Canadian fire data and perimeters being used for this demonstration,
this methodology was specifically designed to be applied to any region. For example,
Well, VIRS and MODIS ActiveFire are global products,
which means wherever the area of interest is,
there is a firm's download that can provide you with the hotspot data.
That being said, we didn't limit this code to processing only firm's data either.
By some slight modifications to the data input functions
to recognize data from an alternative source,
all the rest of the methodology could be applied to any data set
that is featuring ActiveFire stored as point data.

So as long as the practitioner has access to hotspot source, as well as some perimeters or just ways to confirm the presence or absence of an active fire, there is no reason to be focusing on firms downloaded hotspots or Canadian fire perimeters.
You can go ahead and apply this to whatever problem you have that fits the parameters of fire point data.
The main intent for this tutorial was to pass on the theoretical knowledge, which we've contained
mainly within the chapter discussing deep learning, fire, Bayesian neural nets, as well as some
practical coding knowledge, how to bring these models to life, which are going to be shown in
the notebook, which is what I will walk through you today. Our aim was to give a user just a
reliable starting place for designing your own model to be able to address your own problem
regarding active fire detection. The deep learning library that's going to be featured in this
tutorial is Keras and the work of this tutorial is heavily based off of the Keras Bayesian Neural
Networks tutorial. That being said, you do not have to be familiar with that tutorial or even
have worked with Keras at all in order to use our code. The tutorial that we have created for you
starts all the way at the beginning with setting up the environment, installing the packages.
Another point of note is that the original Keras tutorial was written for Keras 2.
At the time of recording this, Keras 3 is now the default version, so some of those Keras 2
functions are depreciated and that tutorial will not run as expected. Therefore, we have gone ahead
and updated all of the functions to be compatible with the Keras 3 environment. Another modification
that we made is in the tutorial, they were using a regression analysis. The data we are going to
to present to you, the presence absence of fire data, is more suited for a classification analysis.
In order to make the code run a classification analysis, some minor changes were done to model
architecture as well as the loss values used by the model. If a difference between a regression
and a classification analysis is something that interests you, I invite you to compare the codes
with an R function to the codes in that tutorial, and a link to that tutorial is provided right
within the notebook. We did our best to preserve the function names between their naming structure
and our naming structure to allow you to perform these kind of comparisons and get to know
the code in a deeper level. In addition to these modifications, we have also included
some novel functions to expand on the abilities not featured in the original. These include
the inclusion of additional model architectures. We've included the functions and the pipeline
to be able to perform hyperparameter tuning. We've included some additional evaluation metrics
and even figures to generate more in-depth evaluations, as well as to give you a more
detailed interpretation of exactly what your model might be doing.
In order to really demonstrate to you the different aspects of this code, we designed two
case studies. Each case study is both a representation of a real application potential
that deep learning Bayesian neural nets could have regarding active fire,
but they're also set up in a way to really demonstrate to the user
how to walk through the design of a deep learning problem,
about how decisions that are made can impact the results.
Through this tutorial, we're going to be using three different data sets.
We're going to be training two different versions of the Bayesian neural net,
and at the end, there'll be five unique deep learning models.

Brief overview, the goal of case study A was just to see if a supervised Bayesian neural network can be used to predict whether satellite-detected hotspots can be correlated to confirmed fires on the ground.

And the purpose of this was to serve as a representation for the user as to how much effort put into dataset preparation, model structure, hyperparameter tuning can affect the model performance down the line.
The second case study we'll present to you is to test a hypothesis, specifically the hypothesis that the source of reported hotspots,
so whether it was vires detected or motus detected in our case, can impact a trained model's ability to then correctly classify said hotspot.
This will also serve as an example of how having a defined study goal can really influence model selection,
because we're going to be comparing two different variations of the Bayesian neural net, point and probabilistic.
And we're going to demonstrate to you that it's not just about the end result of the model,
but if you choose a model that is particularly suited to your task,
you might be able to use features that that version of the model has that others don't
in order to assist with your validation and interpretation of the results.
